summary: Severe energy inefficiencies detected. The code exhibits O(n²) algorithmic complexity where O(n) suffices, pathological I/O patterns (open/close per row), busy-wait polling with no backoff, and eager file loading. Estimated energy waste: 5-50x over typical efficient implementations depending on input size.

hotspots.issue: Quadratic O(n²) nested loop performing linear scan for parent-child matching on every record pair
hotspots.energy_impact: For N records, performs N² comparisons. At 10K records this is ~100M iterations. Each iteration calls dict lookups and string concat.
hotspots.recommendation: Build a dict mapping parent_id → list of children in O(n), then iterate once to build results in O(n). Reduces to O(n) time.

hotspots.issue: String concatenation with += inside a hot loop creates O(n²) allocations — each += copies the entire growing string
hotspots.energy_impact: Quadratic memory churn. Each iteration allocates and copies O(current_length) bytes, triggering GC pressure and cache misses.
hotspots.recommendation: Collect fragments in a list[str] and use ''.join() once after the loop. O(n) allocation.

hotspots.issue: File opened and closed once per row in append mode — O(n) syscalls for open/close/write
hotspots.energy_impact: Each open() triggers a kernel syscall, file handle allocation, and disk metadata update. At 50K rows this is 50K unnecessary syscalls.
hotspots.recommendation: Open the file once before the loop, write all rows, then close after the loop. Use a single 'with open(...) as f:' block.

hotspots.issue: readlines() loads the entire file into memory as a list before iterating
hotspots.energy_impact: Doubles memory for the file contents: one copy as a list of strings, then another as parsed JSON objects. Increases cache pressure and page faults.
hotspots.recommendation: Iterate directly over the file object: for line in f: — this is lazy and streams line-by-line.
hotspots.estimated_speedup: ~1.5-2x memory reduction, varies with file size

hotspots.issue: No exception handling around file I/O or JSON parsing — a single malformed line crashes the entire pipeline, wasting all prior work
hotspots.energy_impact: Wasted energy from partial processing when errors occur. Forces full reprocessing from scratch.
hotspots.recommendation: Wrap file open in try/except and add per-line try/except for json.loads with error logging.
hotspots.estimated_speedup: N/A (reliability, prevents energy waste on retries)

hotspots.issue: Active polling with while True and fixed 100ms sleep — CPU wakes up 10 times/sec even when status is unchanged for long periods
hotspots.energy_impact: 10 context switches/sec × wall-clock time. For a 60-second wait this is 600 unnecessary wake-ups burning CPU time.
hotspots.recommendation: Implement exponential backoff (e.g. 0.1s → 0.2s → 0.4s → ... up to a max of 5-10s), or use an event-driven callback/notification mechanism.
hotspots.estimated_speedup: ~50-90% reduction in CPU time during polling

hotspots.issue: Result initialized as empty string and grown incrementally — see HOTSPOT-002 for the deeper issue, but the empty-string initialization pattern itself is a code smell for concatenation-heavy code.
hotspots.recommendation: Initialize as empty list [] and use ''.join() at the end.

hotspots.issue: Using list.append() in a loop when a list comprehension would be more efficient. Also, storing all parsed objects in a flat list loses parent-child relationships, which then forces HOTSPOT-001's O(n²) search later.
hotspots.energy_impact: list.append() has amortized O(1) but has overhead; the structural issue is the missing index.
hotspots.recommendation: Use a list comprehension: data = [json.loads(line) for line in f]. Also build a parent_id → children index during load to eliminate the O(n²) search.
hotspots.estimated_speedup: ~10-20% on load, combined with HOTSPOT-001 fix yields O(n) overall

hotspots.issue: Module 'csv' imported but never used. Also 'os' imported but never used.
hotspots.energy_impact: Negligible for runtime, but adds ~0.1ms to startup and clutters the namespace.
hotspots.recommendation: Remove unused imports: csv and os.

hotspots.issue: No if __name__ == '__main__': guard — module-level code would run on import if any existed. Currently none, but it's a fragility pattern.
hotspots.energy_impact: None currently, but prevents future accidental energy waste.
hotspots.recommendation: Wrap any future top-level execution code in if __name__ == '__main__':.

prioritized_issues.rationale: Dominant energy cost: O(n²) algorithm dominates all other costs for any n > ~1000. Fixing this yields the single largest energy savings.
String concat in hot loop amplifies the O(n²) cost with O(n²) memory allocation. Should be fixed whenever the loop is touched.
I/O syscall overhead per row is pathological. File open/close is ~10-100μs each; at scale this dwarfs compute costs.
Busy-wait burns CPU continuously over wall-clock time. Exponential backoff is a trivial fix with immediate CPU savings.
Memory doubling from readlines() wastes RAM and increases cache misses. Easy one-line fix.
Restructuring load to build an index enables the HOTSPOT-001 fix and reduces total complexity from O(n²) to O(n).

recommendations: HIGHEST IMPACT: Rewrite process_records to use a dict index (parent_id → children) reducing O(n²) to O(n). Replace += with list + ''.join(). Expected energy reduction: 90-99% for large inputs.
HIGH IMPACT: Refactor export_report to open the output file once before the row loop instead of once per row. Expected energy reduction: 99%+ for I/O time at scale.
MEDIUM IMPACT: Replace busy-wait polling in poll_status with exponential backoff (start 100ms, double up to 5s max). Expected energy reduction: 50-90% of polling CPU time.
LOW IMPACT: Change readlines() to direct file iteration in load_data. Also add try/except for file and JSON errors. Use a list comprehension.
MINOR: Remove unused imports (csv, os). Add __name__ guard for future code.

emissions_estimate.pattern: O(n²) algorithm + O(n) file-open syscalls + busy-wait polling
emissions_estimate.carbon_intent: Analysis only — no code was modified
